chunk_summary: Guidance on fibre intake in children, tailored by nutritional phenotype, with age-specific targets and strategies for safe escalation to improve stooling, appetite, and growth outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific dietary fibre targets for children and adolescents.
Learn how to adjust fibre intake based on nutritional phenotypes such as constipation, faltering growth, or bloating.
Identify practical dietary strategies for increasing fibre intake in culturally relevant ways.
Recognize the importance of pairing fibre increases with adequate fluid intake.
key_facts: Dietary fibre targets: 19 g/day (1–3 years), 25 g/day (4–8 years), 31 g/day (9–13 years boys), 26 g/day (9–13 years girls), 38 g/day (14–18 years males), 26 g/day (14–18 years females).
Fibre escalation in constipation with normal weight: prioritize pulses, vegetables, fruits, and whole grains with increased water intake.
In faltering growth, prioritize calorie-dense foods (e.g., thicker foods, added oils) before cautiously increasing fibre.
Avoid abrupt fibre increases in cases of bloating, pain, or restrictive eating; consider functional abdominal pain or carbohydrate malabsorption.
Practical fibre upgrades in Indian diets: add one extra katori of dal/chana, use whole wheat/millets, and include fruits like guava or papaya.
Always pair fibre increases with adequate fluid to prevent hard stools and reassess within 2–4 weeks.
reference_quote: Use DRI Adequate Intake targets as teaching anchors: 1–3 years 19 g/day; 4–8 years 25 g/day; 9–13 years 31 g/day for boys and 26 g/day for girls; 14–18 years 38 g/day for males and 26 g/day for females.
document_key_topics: Fibre escalation strategies by nutritional phenotype
Practical dietary modifications for fibre intake
Fluid pairing and reassessment in fibre interventions
